title: AI Red Lines — What Never to Do
subtitle: The full network list of things you must not do with AI at an Eduversal partner school, in plain English

_meta.purpose: One page. The full list of red lines for AI use in Eduversal schools. Every role's playbook has a short red-flag section. This file is the long version — the place to check when you're not sure if something crosses a line.
_meta.writingNote: Plain English. Short sentences. Real examples. If a teacher reads a rule three times and still isn't sure what it means, the rule is wrong, not the teacher.

howToUseThisPage.ifInDoubt: Read the rule. Read the 'what this looks like in real life' examples. If you still aren't sure, ask your Subject Leader or Academic Coordinator. The answer is never 'just try it and see'.
howToUseThisPage.noContextShifts: These are NOT 'it depends' rules. They are 'never' rules. If someone tells you a workaround, they are wrong. Check with leadership before acting.
howToUseThisPage.ifSomeoneElseDoesIt: If you see a colleague crossing a red line, talk to them first if it's safe to do so. If it's serious or repeated, raise it to your line manager. The point is not to punish — the point is to protect the school and the students.

theTenRedLines.shortName: Never enter student personal data in public AI tools
theTenRedLines.whoThisIsFor: Everyone who works with student records
theTenRedLines.theRule: Don't type a student's name, photo, school ID, parent contact, medical note, SEN diagnosis, or behaviour incident into a free or personal-account AI tool. Ever.
whatThisLooksLikeInRealLife: BAD: 'Help me write a report comment for Andi Pratama, Year 8, who got 65% on his maths test and has trouble focusing in class.'
BAD: Uploading a photo of a class list to an AI tool to make seating arrangements.
BAD: Pasting parent email replies into AI to draft a response.
GOOD: 'Help me write a report comment for a Year 8 student who scored at the lower end of the class and struggles with attention.' (No names, no specifics.)
theTenRedLines.whyThisMatters: Indonesia's UU PDP No. 27/2022 became fully enforceable in October 2024. The school is responsible for what you type. The school can be fined. The Lembaga PDP enforcement body is starting work in 2026 and is already asking for documentation.
whatYouCanDoInstead: Use anonymous descriptions: 'a Year 7 student who is EAL', 'a student with dyslexia'.
Use only school-approved AI tools (the school has a contract that protects your data).
If you are not sure whether a tool is approved, ask your Academic Coordinator before using it for any student work.

theTenRedLines.shortName: Never let AI grade work that goes into the report
theTenRedLines.theRule: If a mark, score, or grade ends up on a report card or in Cambridge coursework, a human teacher gives the mark. AI does not.
whatThisLooksLikeInRealLife: BAD: Pasting 30 student essays into AI and using the AI's scores for end-of-term reports.
BAD: Letting AI mark Cambridge IGCSE coursework drafts.
BAD: Using AI to rank students from highest to lowest based on their work.
GOOD: Asking AI to draft a feedback comment template that you then adapt for each student.
GOOD: Asking AI to draft a rubric, which you then moderate with your department before using.
theTenRedLines.whyThisMatters: AI sees what a student wrote. It doesn't see what the student understood. The marks AI gives may look reasonable but they are pattern-matching, not judgement. For Cambridge work, the rules are even stricter — see the Cambridge Exams Officers Guide Phase 3.
whatYouCanDoInstead: Use AI to make your marking faster, not to do your marking. Examples: rubric drafts, comment templates, feedback structure suggestions.
For Cambridge coursework, always follow Cambridge's own AI guidance, not just the school's general rules.

theTenRedLines.shortName: Never let AI write the final version of formal assessment items
theTenRedLines.whoThisIsFor: HQ Subject Specialists most of all, but every teacher should know this rule
theTenRedLines.theRule: Chapter tests, EASE items, Cambridge mock papers — these are written by humans. AI can help draft. AI cannot write the final version that goes to students.
whatThisLooksLikeInRealLife: BAD: Asking AI to write 20 chapter test questions and uploading them straight to the formal item bank.
BAD: Letting AI generate the final wording of an EASE item.
BAD: Using AI to create a Cambridge mock paper without expert review of every question.
GOOD: Using AI to draft 20 candidate questions, then a specialist reviews each one, edits, validates against the syllabus, and decides which ones get into the formal bank.
GOOD: Using AI to suggest distractor improvements for an existing item, then a specialist decides.
theTenRedLines.whyThisMatters: AI-written questions look fine but often have wrong answers, weak distractors, or cultural assumptions that don't fit Indonesian students. If a bad item goes into the formal bank, it affects every Eduversal school. The damage is network-wide.
whatYouCanDoInstead: Practice questions for student self-study can use AI more freely — but practice items NEVER feed into formal grading.
Internal drafts and brainstorming are fine. The final form needs a human author.
theTenRedLines.provenance: Eduversal monorepo Common Mistake #33 (practice never feeds formal) + #38 (practice_assessments boundary)

theTenRedLines.shortName: Never send AI text to parents without editing it in your own voice
theTenRedLines.theRule: If AI drafts a parent message, you edit at least a third of it before sending. Your voice has to be in there.
whatThisLooksLikeInRealLife: BAD: Copy-pasting an AI-drafted parent email word-for-word.
BAD: Sending generic-sounding school-wide letters that AI clearly wrote.
GOOD: Using AI to draft a structure, then rewriting in your own words. Adding one detail only you would know.
GOOD: Using AI for first-pass Bahasa Indonesia translation, then reading it aloud and fixing what doesn't sound natural.
theTenRedLines.whyThisMatters: Parents notice generic AI tone fast. After one or two messages, they stop trusting that you actually care. When that trust is gone, every future message has less weight.
whatYouCanDoInstead: Use AI to draft. Use your voice to send.
For Bahasa Indonesia messages, the LLM-generated version is a starting point, not the final text. Indonesian parents expect a more formal register than the LLM tends to default to.

theTenRedLines.shortName: Never use AI to make a decision about a real child
theTenRedLines.theRule: If the question is 'what should we do about this student?' — set placement, intervention plan, SEN referral, discipline, pastoral concern — the answer doesn't come from AI.
whatThisLooksLikeInRealLife: BAD: 'My Year 9 student is struggling — describe to AI and ask for an intervention plan.'
BAD: 'Should this student be moved to a higher set? Let AI decide.'
BAD: Using AI to draft a discipline letter and treating its suggestions as the school's decision.
GOOD: Using AI to summarise notes you've collected on a student's progress, then taking that summary into a meeting with colleagues to decide together.
GOOD: Asking AI to suggest general intervention strategies for a type of difficulty, then choosing which fits THIS student based on your knowledge of them.
theTenRedLines.whyThisMatters: AI doesn't know your students. Even when you describe them in detail, the output is matching against generic patterns, not making a judgement about this real child. The decision needs the people who actually see the child every day — you, your colleagues, the family.
whatYouCanDoInstead: AI summarises evidence you've gathered. You make the decision with the right people in the room.
Pastoral and SEN concerns go through your school's safeguarding and SEN structures, not through a chat window.

theTenRedLines.shortName: Never use your personal AI account for ongoing school work
theTenRedLines.theRule: Free ChatGPT, free Claude, free Gemini, your own paid personal account — these are not for school work that touches student data.
whatThisLooksLikeInRealLife: BAD: Using your personal ChatGPT subscription to draft lesson plans that include real class information.
BAD: Logging into Claude on your phone to mark practice work between lessons.
BAD: Using a 'free trial' of an AI tool with student names.
GOOD: Using only the AI tool the school has approved and signed a contract for. (Ask your Academic Coordinator if you're not sure which one that is.)
theTenRedLines.whyThisMatters: Free and personal accounts usually train their model on what you type. School-tier products (Claude for Education, ChatGPT for Teachers, Gemini for Education) have a contract that says they won't. This is the difference between your students' data being safe and your students' data being used to train a model.
whatYouCanDoInstead: Wait for the school to confirm which AI tool you should use. Use only that one for student work.
Personal-account AI is fine for your own training, your own reading, your own non-student work.

theTenRedLines.shortName: Never use AI to write or score staff appraisals
theTenRedLines.theRule: KPI scoring, Appraisal v2.1 ratings, Principal evaluation — these are done by humans. AI does not draft, score, or finalise any part of staff evaluation.
whatThisLooksLikeInRealLife: BAD: Pasting a teacher's lesson plan notes into AI and asking for an appraisal score.
BAD: Using AI to write the comments in a staff appraisal form.
BAD: Letting AI draft a 360-degree feedback summary.
GOOD: Using AI to help you organise your own notes before writing an appraisal yourself.
GOOD: Using AI to summarise long documents you need to read before an appraisal meeting.
theTenRedLines.whyThisMatters: Staff evaluation is the highest-stakes human judgement a leader makes. Mixing AI into it makes the process unfair, and it puts staff records — which are also personal data under UU PDP — into a third-party AI tool.
whatYouCanDoInstead: AI helps you prepare. You write the appraisal.
If you need to read a lot of documents before an appraisal, AI can summarise them so you can read faster. The judgement is yours.

theTenRedLines.shortName: Never write school AI policy that allows more than Cambridge does
theTenRedLines.theRule: Your school's AI policy can be stricter than Cambridge International's. It cannot be looser. If Cambridge says no, the school says no.
whatThisLooksLikeInRealLife: BAD: 'Cambridge says no AI in coursework, but we allow it for first drafts as long as the final draft is the student's own.' (This contradicts Cambridge — don't.)
BAD: Writing a school policy that doesn't mention Cambridge's coursework AI rules at all.
GOOD: 'For all Cambridge coursework, we follow Cambridge International's guidance in the Exams Officers Guide Phase 3. The school does not permit AI use beyond what Cambridge allows.'
GOOD: Adding school-level rules on top of Cambridge (e.g. requiring disclosure even when Cambridge doesn't).
theTenRedLines.whyThisMatters: Cambridge is the authority on Cambridge coursework. If a school policy contradicts Cambridge, every student's Cambridge work is at risk. The school can lose its Cambridge partnership.
whatYouCanDoInstead: Make sure your Cambridge Coordinator reads every line of school AI policy that touches coursework.
When Cambridge updates its guidance, update the school policy within the same term.

theTenRedLines.shortName: Never accept AI-generated Indonesian facts without checking them
theTenRedLines.whoThisIsFor: HQ Specialists especially, but everyone who teaches Indonesian content
theTenRedLines.theRule: When AI gives you specific Indonesian information — a place name, a historical date, a name of a person, a regulation reference, a local custom — check it before you use it. AI makes these up.
whatThisLooksLikeInRealLife: BAD: Using an AI-drafted scheme of work that names Indonesian historical figures and dates, without checking each one.
BAD: Letting AI include 'Permendiknas No. 45/2023' in a policy draft without checking the regulation exists.
BAD: Including AI-generated examples set in 'Pasar Sentral, Jakarta' without checking the place exists by that name.
GOOD: Treating every AI-generated Indonesian specific as something to verify. Use AI for English structure and framing; verify Indonesian content against authoritative sources.
theTenRedLines.whyThisMatters: AI confabulates — it makes up things that sound plausible. For Indonesian content, this is a common failure mode. Materials with fake Indonesian names and dates damage Eduversal's credibility immediately. Students notice.
whatYouCanDoInstead: Use AI for English-language structure and framing.
Verify Indonesian specifics manually — names, dates, regulation numbers, place names, cultural facts.
When in doubt, leave the specific out and add a [VERIFY] placeholder for yourself.
appliesTo: subject_teacher (humanities, language, sciences with local examples)
theTenRedLines.provenance: manifest:knownGaps.indonesianClassroomCulturalBiasStudy + Eduversal monorepo §19 Latihan import (similar lesson on AI confabulation)

theTenRedLines.shortName: Never inflate your AI competency self-assessment or your school's maturity assessment
theTenRedLines.whoThisIsFor: Everyone (teacher self-assessment) and school leaders (institutional maturity)
theTenRedLines.theRule: If you are Foundation level, mark Foundation. If your school is Level 2, mark Level 2. Inflated ratings hurt you twice.
whatThisLooksLikeInRealLife: BAD: Marking yourself as Practitioner when you only use AI for one workflow occasionally.
BAD: Marking your school as Level 4 on Staff Capability when only 30% of staff use AI confidently.
GOOD: Honest Foundation rating with two specific growth goals for the year.
GOOD: Honest Level 2 institutional rating with two specific domains chosen for improvement.
theTenRedLines.whyThisMatters: Two reasons. First, your line manager (for teacher self-assessment) or the Eduversal Academic Directorate (for school maturity) will compare your rating against evidence and against other schools — inflation gets caught. Second, when you say you're at a level you're not at, you set yourself up to be expected to do things you can't do.
whatYouCanDoInstead: Mark where you are. Plan honest, realistic progress for the year.
If you are unsure of your level, ask your Subject Leader or Academic Coordinator before submitting.

quickCheckBeforeYouUseAi._purpose: When you're about to use AI for a task and you're not sure if it's OK — run through these five questions. If any answer is 'yes', stop.

questions.question: Am I about to type a real student's name, photo, or specific personal information?
Will the AI output go directly onto a report card or into Cambridge work without me marking it myself?
Am I using a personal AI account or a free trial, not a school-approved tool?
questions.ifYes: Stop. Use the school-approved tool only. (Red line 6.)

questions.question: Will this AI output make a decision about a specific student that affects their school experience?
questions.ifYes: Stop. Decisions about real students go through real people. (Red line 5.)

questions.question: Am I writing about a colleague's performance, an appraisal, or a 360 feedback?
questions.ifYes: Stop. Staff evaluation is human-only. (Red line 7.)

quickCheckBeforeYouUseAi.ifAllAreNo: You're probably fine. Use the relevant prompt from prompt-library.json, follow the workflow in your role playbook, and remember to review the AI output before using it.

whatToDoIfYouSeeARedLineCrossed._purpose: If you see a colleague (or yourself, in hindsight) cross one of these lines.
ifYouCrossedItYourself: First, stop using whatever AI output came from it.
Then, tell your Subject Leader or Academic Coordinator. Honest disclosure is much better than discovery later.
If student data was involved, the school's data protection process applies — your leadership will handle it.
Nobody is going to fire you for an honest mistake disclosed quickly. People do get fired for hidden ones.
ifYouSeeColleagueDoIt: First, if it's safe and reasonable, talk to them directly. Many crossings are accidental — the colleague didn't know.
If it's serious (real student data, a grade decision, a parent communication going out unedited) or repeated, raise it with your line manager.
The point is to protect the school and the students, not to punish the colleague. Frame it that way.
ifTheRedLineIsBeingCrossedBySchoolLeader: Raise it with your Academic Coordinator or Cambridge Coordinator first.
If they are the ones crossing it, raise it with the Foundation Representative.
If the issue is network-wide, the Eduversal Academic Directorate is the next step.
